turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: We can identify this group of - go verbs and expect them to go to - ga when they are in the subjunctive or the mood tense. So I leave from salir is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salgo. And if I want to tell somebody leave (formally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salga. And if I want to tell more than one person:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, and if I want to tell them don't leave:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No salgan. Good. Poner was to put. I put:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Pongo, good. Lets speak formally, put it here, put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: And where does that lo go with positive commands?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Póngalo aquí. But don't put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. No lo ponga aquí. We saw with regular verbs that we have a shortcut where we don't use the mood tense for informal positive commands. So if I want to tell you to eat I can just say come, I go to comes and I drop the s, come. If I want to tell you to find it I can go to encontrar,. That’s our shortcut for informal positive commands encuentras, I can drop the s, encuéntralo where we don't use the mood tense. With these - go verbs, to make that shortcut you just drop the - go, so for example, how do you say I have from tener?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tengo. If you want to tell somebody informally have, you just drop the - go:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ten. Have it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes. Tenlo.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: For formal, but we are now looking at the shortcut that we have for informal, so how would you say have it, speaking formally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Téngalo. But informally we are not using the mood tense, we have a short cut, it's just different to the short cut we learnt before, the short cut is just dropping the - go, tenlo. A nice way to remember this is how you will see people in many Spanish speaking countries answering the phone, either dígame, formally, or dime informally. What is I come?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: And how do you tell somebody formally to come?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Venga. Now how would you make it informal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ven. Good. So I should mention that the venga that you hear in Spain as come on is not necessarily formal, it’s lost that feeling of formality because its like a fixed expression, so venga, venga you hear all the time, and then ven will sound much more like come, physically come, rather than come on; venga, venga, venga is used like come on. What was to leave or to go out?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salir. I leave or I go out:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salgo. Leave, speaking informally:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sal. We can just drop the - go. I say or I tell is digo. If you want to say tell me formally, how is it?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Dígame. If you want to make that same command informally, how will it be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Dime. Good. So both things you hear when people answer the phone in Spain - dime, dígame - and in other countries, in other Spanish speaking countries as well. So for - go verbs they will go to - ga to form the mood tense and for the orders, the short cut for the informal positive order they will just drop this - go. So if you want to say tell me when you come, speaking informally, how would that be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. After cuando we are using the mood tense because we are referring to the future. Dime cuando vengas. Tell me when you come. How would you say tell me when you do it - and lets speak formally now? Tell me when you do it, speaking formally:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. Dígame cuando lo haga. Good.
